revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:飯縄権現]][[zh:飯繩權現]]
{{Infobox Deity | name = Izuna Gongen | image = [[File:Izuna Gongen the circumstancial appearance of Mount Izuna.jpg|150px|Izuna Gongen (Guimet Museum)]] | deity_of = Kami of {{ill|Hakusan Faith|ja|白山信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Faith|qq=}}, {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} | associated_with = {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}}, {{ill|Dakini|en|Dakini|qid=Q1127224|lt=Dakiniten|1=Dakini}}, {{ill|Karura|en|Karura|qid=Q82198|lt=Karura|1=Karura}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} | other_names = Iizuna Gongen, Izuna Myōjin | related_shrines = {{ill|Iizuna Shrine|ja|飯縄神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuna Shrine|1=Iizuna Shrine (Q17238950)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17238950}}, {{ill|Takaosan Yakuōin|ja|高尾山薬王院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaosan Yakuōin|1=Takao-san Yakuō-in Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8194732}}, {{ill|Jinno-ji (Kimitsu)|ja|神野寺 (君津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinno-ji (Kimitsu)|1=Jin'ya-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11590963}}, {{ill|Iizuna-dera|ja|飯縄寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuna-dera|1=Izuna-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109597223}}, {{ill|Rinnō-ji|en|Rinnō-ji|qid=Q1348249|lt=Rinnō-ji|1=Rinnō-ji}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}} }}
'''Izuna Gongen''' (飯縄権現, ''Izuna Gongen'') is a syncretic deity associated with the {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} of {{ill|Mount Iizuna|en|Mount Iizuna|qid=Q11666652|lt=Mount Izuna|1=Mount Iizuna}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, Japan. It originated from a fusion of {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} and {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}.
== Overview ==
Izuna Gongen is often depicted as a {{ill|Karasu Tengu|ja|烏天狗|1=karasu tengu|2=ja|3=烏天狗|4=de|5=Karasu Tengu|6=ko|7=가라스텐구|8=es|9=Karasu Tengu|10=sv|11=Karasutengu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karasu Tengu|13=User:Immanuelle/Karasu Tengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q863869}}, riding a white fox entwined with a serpent while wielding a sword and rope. It has been widely worshipped as a war deity, particularly by military leaders such as {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|qid=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|qid=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan|1=Hosokawa clan}} (notably {{ill|Hosokawa Masamoto|en|Hosokawa Masamoto|qid=Q5365082|lt=Hosokawa Masamoto|1=Hosokawa Masamoto}}), {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|qid=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|1=Uesugi Kenshin}}, and [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The famous crest on Uesugi Kenshin’s helmet features an image of Izuna Gongen.
However, the ritual practice associated with Izuna Gongen, known as "Izuna-hō," was often considered a form of esoteric sorcery ({{ill|Gehō|ja|外法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gehō|1=gehou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11430488}}). Medieval sources such as ''Chisō Manroku'' and ''Yamato Honzō'' described it as a form of black magic. Despite this reputation, Izuna Gongen remains an object of devotion at {{ill|Iizuna Shrine|ja|飯縄神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuna Shrine|1=Iizuna Shrine (Q17238950)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17238950}}, {{ill|Takaosan Yakuōin|ja|高尾山薬王院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaosan Yakuōin|1=Takao-san Yakuō-in Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8194732}}, {{ill|Jinno-ji (Kimitsu)|ja|神野寺 (君津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinno-ji (Kimitsu)|1=Jin'ya-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11590963}}, {{ill|Iizuna-dera|ja|飯縄寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iizuna-dera|1=Izuna-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109597223}}, and {{ill|Rinnō-ji|en|Rinnō-ji|qid=Q1348249|lt=Rinnō-ji|1=Rinnō-ji}}, particularly in the Kantō and northern Japan. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Yakuōin was patronized by the {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 徳川氏}}.
<gallery> File:Izuna Gongen.png|Izuna Gongen as depicted in ''Illustrated Buddhist Images'' (1783) File:Haramaki armour of Uesugi Kenshin.jpg|''Various-colored Haramaki'' (Uesugi Shrine), featuring Izuna Gongen on the crest File:Tokaosan-Yakuouin.jpg|Gongen-dō Hall at Takaosan Yakuōin File:Izna-gongen.jpg|Statue of Izuna Gongen at Takaosan Yakuōin </gallery>
== Origins ==
The origins of Izuna Gongen worship vary across different legends and religious texts.
Although Mount Izuna is part of the foothills of {{ill|Mount Togakushi|en|Mount Togakushi|qid=Q11496659|lt=Mount Togakushi|1=Mount Togakushi}}, Izuna Shugendō developed under the larger {{ill|Togakushi Shugendō|ja|戸隠修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togakushi Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tradition. Some sources claim that Izuna worship predates Togakushi worship.<ref>{{harvp|Muramura|1987|p=57}}</ref>
According to historical records, a hermit known as Gakumon-gyōja practiced asceticism on Mount Izuna in 849 (''Kashō'' 2) before later founding the Togakushi temple complex.<ref>{{harvp|Chikiri|1975|p=393}}</ref>
A document from 1458, the ''Togakushi-san Kenkō-ji Ryūki'' (戸隠山顕光寺流記), describes an event in 1233 (''Tempuku'' 1), in which Izuna Daimyōjin appeared to the abbot of Togakushi and proclaimed, "I am Japan’s third greatest Tengu." Izuna Gongen was then enshrined at Togakushi as a protective deity.<ref>{{harvp|Muramura|1987|p=57}}</ref>
Another Edo-period text, the ''Izuna-san Ryakuengi'', states that in 848 (''Kashō'' 1), Gakumon-gyōja witnessed the divine manifestation of Izuna Myōjin. Later, in 1233, Lord Itō Bizen-no-kami Tadatsuna of Ogino Castle received a divine oracle from Izuna Gongen and dedicated rituals to it.<ref>{{harvp|Mizusawa|2010}}</ref>
The systematization of Izuna worship likely took place during the early medieval period, with the oldest extant icon of Izuna Gongen dating back to 1406 (''Ōei'' 13). Several paintings from the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} and an {{ill|Izuna Mandala|ja|飯縄曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izuna Mandala|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579528|1=Izuna Mandala}} associated with Rinnō-ji suggest an established tradition by that time.
== Development ==
Izuna worship evolved in multiple directions, including: * Tengu worship * War deity veneration among samurai * Esoteric practices among Shugendō monks * Associations with ninja mysticism * Fox spirit beliefs
By the Muromachi period, it had already been perceived as a form of magic or esoteric arts.
== Mantras ==
Several mantras are associated with Izuna Gongen:
* '''On Chira Chira Ya Sowaka''' ** "Chira Chira Ya" is derived from Izuna Chira Tengu. ** The ''Konjaku Monogatari'' mentions a tengu named Chira Eiju.
* '''On Kirikaku Sowaka''' ** Used at Takaosan Yakuōin. ** Although typically associated with {{ill|Dakini|en|Dakini|qid=Q1127224|lt=Dakiniten|1=Dakini}}, it is recited at Yakuōin for Izuna Gongen. ** Instead, Yakuōin uses '''On Dakini Gyatchi Gyan Kan Ei Sowaka''' for Dakiniten.
== Notes == {{notelist2}}
== References == {{Reflist|30em|refs= <ref name=gorai>{{Citation|和書|last=Gorai |first=Shigeru |author-link=五来重 |title=Shugendō Historical Source Collection |volume=17 |publisher=[[Meicho Publishing]] |year=1983 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pwvmAAAAMAAJ&q=飯縄山略縁起+嘉祥元年 |page=}}</ref>
<ref name=mizusawa>{{Citation|和書|last=Mizusawa |first=Ryūju |title=Traces of Nomadic Peoples (15) - Tengu Legends |journal=Rekishi Yomihon |volume=55 |number=3 |publisher=[[Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha]] |year=2010 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IWJMAQAAIAAJ&q=飯縄山略縁起+嘉祥元年 |page=266}}</ref> }}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Least weasel|en|Least weasel|qid=Q25311|lt=Iizuna|1=Least weasel}} * {{ill|Kuda-gitsune|en|Kuda-gitsune|qid=Q4451648|lt=Kuda-gitsune|1=Kuda-gitsune}} * {{ill|Dakini|en|Dakini|qid=Q1127224|lt=Dakiniten|1=Dakini}} * {{ill|Karura|en|Karura|qid=Q82198|lt=Karura|1=Karura}} * {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} * {{ill|Akiba Gongen|ja|秋葉権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akiba Gongen|qq=}} * {{ill|Shōgun Jizō|ja|将軍地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgun Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地蔵菩薩|1=Kṣitigarbha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q604687}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isumi-kankou.com/isumi-kanko-tousyu/iduna.html Iizuna in Isumi City Tourism Guide] {{Shinto Horizontal}}
